CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the manuscript is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (generative encoder–decoder with a copy mechanism and empirical gains), and the writing quality is described as good with thorough related work and reported hyperparameters. From a presentation standpoint notation appears consistent for an RNN/attention model and limitations and examples (including failure cases) are explicitly discussed, which aids clarity. The summary does not report on the effectiveness of figures/tables or code release; absence of explicit mention of illustrative figures/tables and reproducibility artifacts prevents a top score under my conservative standards. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and clearly presents its contributions early — the CopyRNN idea, training on a large 567k-corpus, and the capability to generate absent keyphrases are stated up front. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent (encoder–decoder, attention, copy mechanism) and hyperparameters and related work are well reported, which supports reproducibility. The writing quality is described as good and the experiments are laid out logically, but the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables and examples are uniformly effective or whether any terse sections exist, so I withhold a top score for minor presentation gaps that may remain.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the manuscript is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (generative encoder–decoder with a copy mechanism and empirical gains), and the writing quality is described as good with thorough related work and reported hyperparameters. From a presentation standpoint notation appears consistent for an RNN/attention model and limitations and examples (including failure cases) are explicitly discussed, which aids clarity. The summary does not report on the effectiveness of figures/tables or code release; absence of explicit mention of illustrative figures/tables and reproducibility artifacts prevents a top score under my conservative standards.

Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent (encoder–decoder, attention, copy mechanism) and hyperparameters and related work are well reported, which supports reproducibility. The writing quality is described as good and the experiments are laid out logically, but the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables and examples are uniformly effective or whether any terse sections exist, so I withhold a top score for minor presentation gaps that may remain.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates a substantial empirical gain (≈20% on present-keyphrase extraction) using a copy-enabled RNN trained on a large corpus (KP20k and 527k training articles), which should shift practitioners toward generative approaches for keyphrase prediction and absent-keyphrase generation. It opens clear new directions (copy/seq2seq for extraction, large supervised corpora) and is likely to be built upon, but I reserve a top score because the summary indicates limited analysis of failure modes, theoretical justification, and no mention of released code/models—factors that constrain reproducibility and broader theoretical impact. | B(4): This paper addresses an important, practical problem—automatic keyphrase generation—from a modern seq2seq perspective and demonstrates substantial empirical gains (roughly 20% average improvement on present-keyphrase extraction) over strong unsupervised and supervised baselines. The use of a large curated corpus (≈567k articles), attention+copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to handle OOVs and to generate absent keyphrases, and end-to-end training make the approach immediately useful to practitioners and likely to change system design choices in the area. While not necessarily transformative theoretically, the results are strong and broadly applicable, and will likely spur further work on generative keyphrase models and dataset-driven evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates a substantial empirical gain (≈20% on present-keyphrase extraction) using a copy-enabled RNN trained on a large corpus (KP20k and 527k training articles), which should shift practitioners toward generative approaches for keyphrase prediction and absent-keyphrase generation. It opens clear new directions (copy/seq2seq for extraction, large supervised corpora) and is likely to be built upon, but I reserve a top score because the summary indicates limited analysis of failure modes, theoretical justification, and no mention of released code/models—factors that constrain reproducibility and broader theoretical impact.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important, practical problem—automatic keyphrase generation—from a modern seq2seq perspective and demonstrates substantial empirical gains (roughly 20% average improvement on present-keyphrase extraction) over strong unsupervised and supervised baselines. The use of a large curated corpus (≈567k articles), attention+copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to handle OOVs and to generate absent keyphrases, and end-to-end training make the approach immediately useful to practitioners and likely to change system design choices in the area. While not necessarily transformative theoretically, the results are strong and broadly applicable, and will likely spur further work on generative keyphrase models and dataset-driven evaluation.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against a broad set of standard unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf–Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) across multiple established datasets and uses consistent metrics (precision/recall/F1), which supports fair empirical comparison. The authors clearly attribute gains to the copy-enabled RNN and report both strengths and limitations, and the related-work coverage is thorough. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to recent neural or other state-of-the-art learning-based keyphrase models or release of code for reproducibility, so the empirical comparison, while strong, has minor omissions that prevent a fully comprehensive benchmark. | B(4): The paper evaluates CopyRNN against a broad set of standard unsupervised (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank) and supervised (KEA, Maui) baselines across multiple canonical datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) and reports standard P/R/F metrics, which is strong and appropriate. Experiments use a large collected training corpus and held-out set, suggesting fair conditions; hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. The manuscript reports clear large absolute gains (≈20% on present-keyphrase extraction) and discusses limitations/errors, but it does not appear to include comparisons to other recent neural sequence models or contemporary supervised neural extractors that could be relevant — hence minor omissions prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a broad set of standard unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf–Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) across multiple established datasets and uses consistent metrics (precision/recall/F1), which supports fair empirical comparison. The authors clearly attribute gains to the copy-enabled RNN and report both strengths and limitations, and the related-work coverage is thorough. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to recent neural or other state-of-the-art learning-based keyphrase models or release of code for reproducibility, so the empirical comparison, while strong, has minor omissions that prevent a fully comprehensive benchmark.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates CopyRNN against a broad set of standard unsupervised (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank) and supervised (KEA, Maui) baselines across multiple canonical datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval-2010, KP20k) and reports standard P/R/F metrics, which is strong and appropriate. Experiments use a large collected training corpus and held-out set, suggesting fair conditions; hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. The manuscript reports clear large absolute gains (≈20% on present-keyphrase extraction) and discusses limitations/errors, but it does not appear to include comparisons to other recent neural sequence models or contemporary supervised neural extractors that could be relevant — hence minor omissions prevent a top score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work is squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core NLP task (keyphrase generation) with modern sequence models (encoder–decoder with attention and copying) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval, KP20k) plus a large collected corpus. The methods and evaluation (macro P/R/F, comparisons to established baselines like Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA/Maui) align with community expectations, and the audience (NLP researchers working on generation, IR, and metadata extraction) is appropriate. My only mild concern for acceptance would be reproducibility — code release is not mentioned — but that does not change the paper’s topical fit for the venue. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core NLP/IR problem (keyphrase generation) with a contemporary seq2seq+attention model augmented by a practical copy mechanism (CopyRNN) and demonstrates large empirical gains (≈20% over strong baselines) on multiple standard datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval, KP20k) and a very large collected corpus (≈568k articles). The methodology, metrics (precision/recall/F1), and comparisons to both unsupervised and supervised baselines are exactly the empirical rigor this community expects, and the contribution (generating absent keyphrases and handling copying/OOVs) addresses a real-world need. Given the scale of experiments, clear evaluation, and relevance to both NLP and IR researchers, I judge it a strong match for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This work is squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core NLP task (keyphrase generation) with modern sequence models (encoder–decoder with attention and copying) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval, KP20k) plus a large collected corpus. The methods and evaluation (macro P/R/F, comparisons to established baselines like Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA/Maui) align with community expectations, and the audience (NLP researchers working on generation, IR, and metadata extraction) is appropriate. My only mild concern for acceptance would be reproducibility — code release is not mentioned — but that does not change the paper’s topical fit for the venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core NLP/IR problem (keyphrase generation) with a contemporary seq2seq+attention model augmented by a practical copy mechanism (CopyRNN) and demonstrates large empirical gains (≈20% over strong baselines) on multiple standard datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval, KP20k) and a very large collected corpus (≈568k articles). The methodology, metrics (precision/recall/F1), and comparisons to both unsupervised and supervised baselines are exactly the empirical rigor this community expects, and the contribution (generating absent keyphrases and handling copying/OOVs) addresses a real-world need. Given the scale of experiments, clear evaluation, and relevance to both NLP and IR researchers, I judge it a strong match for a top conference.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by extensive experiments on multiple standard datasets and a large proprietary training corpus, showing substantial gains for the copy-enabled RNN over strong unsupervised and supervised baselines. However, I note missing rigorous analysis common to theory-minded reviewers: no formal guarantees, limited ablations isolating each architectural choice (e.g., attention vs. copy), and no mention of statistical significance testing or released code for reproducibility. Mathematical content is minimal but appropriate for an engineering contribution; the empirical methodology is broadly sound in choice of baselines and metrics but would benefit from more controlled experiments and diagnostics of failure modes. Overall the technical claims are credible but suffer from moderate gaps in analysis and reproducibility, so I score 4 (minor issues). | B(4): The paper states clear claims (CopyRNN with attention+copy improves present-keyphrase extraction and can generate absent keyphrases) and supports them with large-scale experiments on multiple standard datasets plus a sizeable 527k training corpus, showing ~20% absolute gains over strong unsupervised/supervised baselines. The model design and training procedure (biGRU encoder, GRU decoder, attention, copy mechanism) are sound and hyperparameters are reported; the empirical comparisons include a broad set of baselines. Minor technical gaps remain — I see no mention of statistical significance testing, public code, or deeper analysis of when copying fails — but these do not undermine the main experimental claims. Overall the evidence convincingly supports the core technical claims, with only modest concerns about reproducibility and statistical rigor.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by extensive experiments on multiple standard datasets and a large proprietary training corpus, showing substantial gains for the copy-enabled RNN over strong unsupervised and supervised baselines. However, I note missing rigorous analysis common to theory-minded reviewers: no formal guarantees, limited ablations isolating each architectural choice (e.g., attention vs. copy), and no mention of statistical significance testing or released code for reproducibility. Mathematical content is minimal but appropriate for an engineering contribution; the empirical methodology is broadly sound in choice of baselines and metrics but would benefit from more controlled experiments and diagnostics of failure modes. Overall the technical claims are credible but suffer from moderate gaps in analysis and reproducibility, so I score 4 (minor issues).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims (CopyRNN with attention+copy improves present-keyphrase extraction and can generate absent keyphrases) and supports them with large-scale experiments on multiple standard datasets plus a sizeable 527k training corpus, showing ~20% absolute gains over strong unsupervised/supervised baselines. The model design and training procedure (biGRU encoder, GRU decoder, attention, copy mechanism) are sound and hyperparameters are reported; the empirical comparisons include a broad set of baselines. Minor technical gaps remain — I see no mention of statistical significance testing, public code, or deeper analysis of when copying fails — but these do not undermine the main experimental claims. Overall the evidence convincingly supports the core technical claims, with only modest concerns about reproducibility and statistical rigor.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work adapts established encoder–decoder, attention, and copying ideas rather than proposing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm, but they also concur the application (generating absent keyphrases), the introduction of CopyRNN, and the large curated corpus represent a meaningful and non-trivial extension with clear empirical gains. Given this balance—solid practical innovation and strong empirical contribution but limited theoretical novelty—I rate originality as above moderate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies established encoder-decoder and attention architectures with a copy mechanism to keyphrase generation—an application-specific but conceptually straightforward adaptation of existing seq2seq techniques rather than a fundamentally new model. Its ability to generate absent keyphrases and the empirical gains over extraction baselines provide useful contributions, and the authors clearly differentiate from prior work by training on a large scientific corpus and introducing CopyRNN. From a theory-focused perspective, however, the methods are a non-trivial engineering extension rather than a novel formal or algorithmic advance, so I rate the originality as moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper adapts sequence-to-sequence generation with attention and a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to the keyphrase generation task, which meaningfully departs from prior extractive methods and enables the novel capability of producing absent (not-in-source) keyphrases. Using a large curated corpus (≈527k training articles) and demonstrating ~20% gains over strong extractive baselines shows the idea is more than a minor tweak. That said, the core modeling components (encoder–decoder, attention, copying) are established techniques, so the contribution is a notable, well-differentiated application/extension rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is strong—large training corpus, multiple standard datasets and baselines, and reported hyperparameters—supporting the main claims. They also agree key weaknesses are limited ablation/sensitivity analyses and lack of clear code/corpus release, which hinder deeper mechanistic insight and full reproducibility. Given the solid experimental results but incomplete diagnostic analyses and artifact release, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents strong empirical comparisons across multiple standard datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval, KP20k) and baselines and reports large gains, supporting the main claim; hyperparameters are reported and a large training corpus was used. However, there is no mention of code release and only limited discussion of failure modes (a couple examples and a 2% OOV stat), which harms reproducibility and diagnostic understanding. Crucially, ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contribution of attention vs. copying vs. dataset/training choices are not described, leaving theoretical and mechanistic claims under-supported. Related work coverage is thorough, but the absence of deeper analysis and released artifacts leads me to judge the experimental depth as adequate but missing important analyses.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the experimental evaluation is strong: the authors train on a large 527k+ article corpus (40k held-out) and compare CopyRNN and the generative RNN to a wide set of classical unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) across five standard datasets with precision/recall/F1 metrics, supporting the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is limited to comparing the generative RNN vs the copy-enabled variant (showing clear gains) but I see no finer-grained ablations (e.g., attention/no-attention, vocabulary/beam sensitivity) that would strengthen understanding. Related work appears thoroughly covered, and the paper candidly discusses failure modes and limitations (overly general outputs, OOV issues ~2%, example erroneous predictions). Reproducibility is reasonably supported by reported hyperparameters and dataset descriptions, though code is not mentioned and the large collected corpus may lack full release details, so complete replication could be hindered.

main_claims: Proposes a generative deep keyphrase generation model (encoder-decoder) for predicting keyphrases from text, named deep keyphrase generation.

Introduces a copy-enabled RNN variant (CopyRNN) that leverages source text copying and significantly outperforms a range of unsupervised and supervised baselines on present-keyphrase extraction (~20% average improvement over best baselines).
The model can generate absent (not present in source) topical keyphrases by capturing deeper semantic meaning, and is trained on a large collected corpus of scientific metadata.
_summary.methodology: Use an RNN encoder-decoder (bidirectional GRU encoder and forward GRU decoder) with attention and a copy mechanism to generate keyphrases from title+abstract; models trained end-to-end and decoded with beam search.
datasets: Collected corpus of 567,830 articles (model trained on 527,830; 40,000 held out)
limitations: Pure generative RNN tends to generate too-general keyphrases and cannot recall out-of-vocabulary words.
About nearly 2% of correct predictions contain out-of-vocabulary words, indicating vocabulary limitations.
CopyRNN still makes erroneous predictions (example: predicts 'rich content' erroneously instead of 'video metadata').

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Tenured NLP researcher with a strong formal-methods background; expertise in sequence models, learning theory, and model interpretability. Has served on program committees for ACL/ICLR and often reviews papers that bridge theory and practice.
traits: Penalises papers that lack theoretical justification: deducts points if no formal motivation, guarantee, or analysis of model behavior is provided.
Penalises papers with limited or non-rigorous ablation/statistical analysis: expects ablations that isolate each architectural choice and significance testing across runs.
Requires clarity on model limitations and failure modes: deducts points when empirical improvements are reported without diagnostic analyses explaining when/why the model succeeds or fails.

Penalises lack of reproducibility materials: expects code, trained models, or precise training details (hyperparameters, seeds) to be released or included in the appendix; will lower score if omitted.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Conservative, focuses on theoretical grounding, careful ablations and reproducibility; will downscore empirical-only gains that lack analysis or explanation.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical ML Practitioner
reviewer_b.background: Senior applied machine learning researcher focused on information retrieval and practical NLP systems; extensive experience building and evaluating sequence-to-sequence models for production tasks and curating large datasets.
traits: Rewards large-scale, empirical improvements: awards points for substantial absolute gains over strong baselines on realistic, large datasets.
Values practical novelty and applicability: favors work that introduces or adapts modeling techniques (e.g., copy mechanism in generation) that improve downstream utility even without formal proofs.
Requires comprehensive baseline comparisons: rewards papers that compare against a wide spectrum of unsupervised and supervised baselines and show consistent improvements across metrics.

Rewards contributions that address real-world needs (e.g., generating absent keyphrases) and the construction/use of sizeable curated corpora; will raise score if dataset collection/scale is well documented and impactful.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and results-oriented: emphasizes empirical rigor, breadth of baselines, and practical impact; willing to accept limited theoretical analysis if experiments are thorough and convincing.
